tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
 - .plt.sec@start: 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_1>>
 - c_print_results(char*, char, int, int, int, int, double, double, char*, int, char*, char*, char*, char*, char*, char*, char*, char*, char*, char*, char*, char*): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_2>>
 - randlc(double*, double): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_3>>

 - timer_start(int): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_6>>
 - timer_stop(int): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_7>>
 - unknown: 20 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_8>>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - JMP: 9 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.
hint.details:
- CLTQ: 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_cvt_1>>
 - VCVTSI2SD (INT32/64 to FP64, scalar): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_cvt_2>>

The binary function is loading 360 bytes (45 double precision FP elements).
The binary function is storing 280 bytes (35 double precision FP elements).

expert.txt:
| ALU0/BRU0 | ALU1  | ALU2  | ALU3/BRU1 | AGU0  | AGU1  | AGU2  | FP0  | FP1  | FP2   | FP3

Execution ports to units layout:
 - ALU0/BRU0: ALU, BRU
 - ALU1: ALU
 - ALU2: ALU
 - ALU3/BRU1: ALU, BRU
 - AGU0 (256 bits): store address, load
 - AGU1 (256 bits): store address, load
 - AGU2: store address
 - FP0 (256 bits): VPU
 - FP1 (256 bits): VPU
 - FP2 (256 bits): VPU, FP store data
 - FP3 (256 bits): VPU, DIV/SQRT

Cycles executing div or sqrt instructions: 5.00-6.50
Cycles loading/storing data              : 40.00

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 88.67 cycles. At this rate:
 - 6% of peak load performance is reached (4.06 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 9% of peak store performance is reached (3.16 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 88.67 to 70.50 cycles (1.26x speedup).

header:
Warnings:
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [ENDBR64] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [CLTQ] is unknown
 - Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions.
Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis  or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.03 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 88.67 to 16.17 cycles (5.48x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Make array accesses unit-stride:
  * If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
8% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 13% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 7% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
 - 50% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
 - 0% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
 - 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
 - 8% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is not vectorized.
Only 21% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 88.67 to 12.98 cycles (6.83x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.00x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt: Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
